title: Flipper (invader) vector chain matches the ROM byte-for-byte, and it is RED
claim: INVA1S (ALDISP.MAC 1907-1916) is an 8-vector chain in unit/perp-unit multipliers: (4,1) (4,-1) (-2,1) (1,1) (-3,-1) (-3,1) (1,-1) (-2,-1). FLIPPER_DELTAS is that exact sequence in that exact order. The deltas sum to (0,0), so the chain closes on itself — our fromDeltas()/closed:true reproduction is equivalent. Colour: ALCOMN.MAC 367 `FLICOL=RED`, applied at ALDISP.MAC 670 `LDA I,FLICOL`; flipperGlyph returns color:'red' (glyphs.ts 86).
reasoning: The single most-seen enemy in the game is vertex-exact and colour-exact. Note the flipper is NOT drawn from ALVROM: the ALVROM `ENEMY PICTURES` block (ENER11..ENER44, lines 1360-1849) sits inside `.IF NE,SPACG` with `SPACG=0` (ALVROM.MAC 14, 1359) and is never assembled — the shipped flipper is this ALDISP point-vector table, drawn between two well points.

title: Player cursor (claw) graphics NCRS1-8 match the ROM byte-for-byte, and they are YELLOW
claim: All eight cursor chains agree vertex-for-vertex with CLAW_DELTAS: NCRS1 (0,-2)(2,-1)(3,4)(-3,-3)(-1,0)(0,2)(2,1)(-3,-1); NCRS2 (1,-2)(7,2)(-3,1)(2,-1)(-6,-1)(0,1)(2,1)(-3,-1); NCRS3 (2,-2)(6,2)...; NCRS4 (3,-2)(5,2)...; NCRS5 (5,-2)(3,2)...; NCRS6 (6,-2)(2,2)...; NCRS7 (7,-2)(1,2)...; NCRS8 opens with the beam-off move `VEC 3,1,0` (ALDISP.MAC 1988) then (3,-4)(2,1)(0,2)(-3,1)(2,-1)(0,-2)(-1,0)(-3,3) — which is exactly our graphic 8, post-MOVE. Colour: ALCOMN.MAC 360 `CURCOL=YELLOW`, set at ALDISP.MAC 602; CLAW_GLYPHS use color:'yellow' (glyphs.ts 263).
reasoning: The claw — the thing the player looks at for the whole game — is exact, including the roll sequence. This is the strongest fidelity result in the audit.

title: Enemy lines (spikes) are a GREEN line capped by a single WHITE dot
claim: The enemy-line body carries the fixed VG prologue `ENLFIX` whose comment is `(CSTATGREEN,CNTR)` (ALDISP.MAC 3027); an un-shattered tip inserts a WHITE colour stat (ALDISP.MAC 3208-3215, WHITIP) then a JSRL to JSRDOT (a zero-length VCTR 0,0 = one dot). drawSpikes strokes the shaft '#39ff14' (render.ts 251) and fills exactly one white tip dot (render.ts 263-266). Line colour and tip colour, and the single-dot cap, all match.
reasoning: Both halves of the spike — the green shaft and the one white cap dot, no sparkle — are right. (The SHATTERED tip is a different picture; see V-020.)

title: Pulsar strobes TURQUOISE when idle and WHITE while pulsing
claim: PULPIC loads TURQOI for pulse-off and WHITE for pulse-on (ALDISP.MAC 861-867), and the pulsar's colour-RAM slot is index 4 = ZTURQOI (ALDISP.MAC 2410). pulsarColor(bright) returns 'white' when bright and 'cyan' otherwise (glyphs.ts 196-198), and GLYPH_HEX.cyan = '#00e5ff' (render.ts 42) — the turquoise/white strobe, same polarity.
reasoning: Our 'cyan' is the palette's turquoise slot; the strobe partner (white) is exactly the ROM's.

title: The pulsar bar's four flatter variants are amplitude-scaled, not the ROM's four hand-authored chains

ours.verbatim: const PULSAR_AMP: readonly number[] = [1, 0.6, 0.35, 0.15, 0] // variant 0 sharpest .. 4 flat

claim: The ROM has five DISTINCT chains (ALDISP.MAC 2001-2035): PULS4 = (2,-3)(1,6)(1,-6)(1,6)(1,-6)(2,3) — 6 drawn vectors; PULS3 = move(1,0) then (1,-2)(1,4)(1,-4)(1,4)(1,-4)(1,2) — 6 drawn vectors, x-stride 1 throughout, amplitude 4; PULS2 = move(1,0) then (1,-1)(1,2)(1,-2)(1,2)(1,-2)(1,1) — amplitude 2; PULS1 = move(1,0) then (2,-1)(2,2)(2,-1) — only THREE drawn vectors; PULS0 = move(1,0) then (6,0) — ONE flat 6-unit vector. Ours keeps one delta table (PULSAR_XD [2,1,1,1,1,2], PULSAR_YD y = -3,6,-6,6,-6,3) — which reproduces PULS4 exactly — and derives the other four by multiplying y by 1/0.6/0.35/0.15/0 (glyphs.ts 164-185): every variant keeps 6 segments and the 2/1/1/1/1/2 x-stride, so PULS1's 3-segment and PULS0's 1-segment forms are never drawn. Selection also inverts at the top: PULTAB clamps index >= 5 to CPULS0 (FLAT) (ALDISP.MAC 875-878, 893), while pulsarVariant() maps index 5 to PULSAR_DP_T1[5]=0x09 → variant 0 (SHARPEST) (glyphs.ts 189-194).
reasoning: Only the sharpest bar is authentic; the intermediate pulse frames and the collapsed/flat frames are ours, and the top-of-range frame is inverted (sharp where the ROM is flat). The pulsar's whole visual 'breath' is those intermediate frames. [REFUTATION] Read ALDISP.MAC:2001-2035 in full. PULS4S (2,-3)(1,6)(1,-6)(1,6)(1,-6)(2,3) = 6 vectors, no leading move — matches. PULS3S/PULS2S both open with 'VEC 1,0,0' (confirmed via the VEC macro at ALDISP.MAC:1883-1898: a literal 3rd argument of 0 sets the beam-on flag ...ONF=0, i.e. move-only, vs the 2-arg form defaulting ...ONF=1/on) then 6 drawn vectors each — matches 'move(1,0) then 6 drawn' for both, with amplitudes 4 and 2 respectively as claimed. PULS1S is 'VEC 1,0,0' + only 3 drawn vectors (2,-1)(2,2)(2,-1) — matches 'only THREE drawn vectors' exactly, a real structural difference (not just smaller amplitude) from PULS4/3/2's six-segment chains. PULS0S is 'VEC 1,0,0' + a single 'VEC 6,0' — the cited line 2034 verbatim matches exactly, confirming the ONE flat vector claim. Confirmed our glyphs.ts (164-185): PULSAR_XD=[2,1,1,1,1,2] and PULSAR_YD reproduce PULS4 exactly, and all 5 'variants' scale the SAME 6-segment/6-vertex chain by PULSAR_AMP — genuinely never producing PULS1's 3-vector or PULS0's 1-vector topology; this is a real fabrication versus the ROM's genuinely distinct chains. Then verified the inversion claim by hand: PULPIC (ALDISP.MAC:868-880) computes index=(PULSON+64)>>4, and when index>=5 it is FORCED TO 0 (not clamped to 5) before indexing PULTAB — PULTAB[0]=CPULS0 (flat). Checked reachability: PULSON bounces in roughly [-63,15] (ALWELG.MAC:1557-1570) but is updated BEFORE the bounds check each frame with PULTIM up to 8 (WPULTIM table), so it can transiently overshoot past 15 (e.g. to ~18-23) on the very frame it turns around — genuinely reachable, not dead code — triggering the >=5 clamp-to-flat in the ROM. Our pulsarVariant (glyphs.ts:191-194) instead does 'Math.min(computed, 5)' — clamping the INDEX itself to 5 rather than zeroing it — and PULSAR_DP_T1[5]=0x09 maps to variant 0 = SHARPEST, the opposite of the ROM's flat. Confirmed pulsarBar/pulsarVariant/pulsarColor are live, called from render.ts:361-364, not dead code. Both halves of the claim hold. [CORRECTION] None.

title: The tanker body is a 4-vertex diamond; the ROM's GENTNK is a 17-vertex double diamond with an internal X
claim: GENTNK (ALVROM.MAC 651-669, CM=2 CD=1) is a chain of SCVEC ABSOLUTE vertices (the CVEC macro, ALVROM.MAC 68-78, emits the delta to each new absolute point). Entering from TANKR's blank move to (20,0) it draws, in hex object units: (20,0) → (0,20) → (0,0C) → (20,0) → (0C,0) → (0,0C) → (-0C,0) → (0,20) → (-20,0) → (-0C,0) → (0,-0C) → (-20,0) → (0,-20) → (0,-0C) → (0C,0) → (0,-20) → (20,0) → (0C,0). Decimal, x2 for CM: an OUTER diamond at (+/-64,0),(0,+/-64), an INNER diamond at (+/-24,0),(0,+/-24), and the eight chords that lace them into an X. Ours is a single 4-point diamond (0,-9)(6,0)(0,9)(-6,0), i.e. only the outer ring, and elongated (9 tall x 6 wide) where the ROM's is square (equal x/y extent). Colour is right: ALCOMN.MAC 368 `TANCOL=PURPLE`, ours color:'purple' (glyphs.ts 97).
reasoning: The tanker is the ROM's most ornate on-well shape and reads at a glance as a boxed, laced diamond. Ours is a plain rhombus — the least faithful of the five enemy bodies. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 624-669. TANKR falls through into GENTNK entering at abs (0x20,0) (post CM=2/CD=1 scaling, (0x40,0)); GENTNK's 17 SCVEC lines are absolute-target vertices (CVEC computes delta = NEWX-OLX against the running OLX/OLZ, so each SCVEC line is a jump to a NEW absolute point, not a relative offset). Hand-traced all 18 points (entry + 17): they resolve to exactly the outer diamond (+/-0x20,0)/(0,+/-0x20) and inner diamond (+/-0xC,0)/(0,+/-0xC) the claim lists, with the outer diamond's 4 edges, the inner diamond's 4 edges, and 8 more connective edges (4 axis retraces + 4 cross-quadrant inner-to-outer diagonals, one axis edge drawn twice) lacing them together — matches 'eight chords... into an X' when the duplicate retrace is deduped. CM=2/CD=1 verified in effect for this whole section (set at 518-520, unchanged until 722). Citation exact: line 651 is verbatim 'GENTNK:\tCSTAT PURPLE'; TANCOL=PURPLE at ALCOMN.MAC 368. Our glyphs.ts:95 diamond has y-extent 18 vs x-extent 12 (elongated 3:2) against the ROM's equal-extent (square-diagonal) double diamond, and is missing the inner diamond and all lacing entirely. Holds on macro-decode, unit, and governing-code grounds.

title: Tanker cargo emblems: the ROM prefixes a TURQOI chevron (pulsars) or a 4-colour bar (fuses), not our cyan zigzag / yellow cross

ours.verbatim: ? { points: [{ x: -3, y: 0 }, { x: -1, y: -2 }, { x: 1, y: 2 }, { x: 3, y: 0 }], closed: false, color: 'cyan' }

claim: TANKP (pulsar tanker, ALVROM.MAC 624-633) prefixes GENTNK with ONE turquoise open chain: blank move to (-5,-2), then draw (-3,6) (0,-6) (3,6) (5,-2) — a W/chevron spanning the body. TANKF (fuse tanker, ALVROM.MAC 634-647) prefixes a FOUR-colour mark: CSTAT BLUE draw to (-0C,0); blank to (0,0C); CSTAT RED dot at (0,0C); CSTAT GREEN draw to (0,-0C); CSTAT YELLOW draw to (0C,0). TANKR (flipper tanker, ALVROM.MAC 648-650) has no mark, and TANTAB confirms the three-way pick (ALDISP.MAC 696 `TANTAB:	.BYTE PTTANK,PTTANK,PTTANP,PTTANF`). Ours: pulsar cargo = a 4-point cyan zigzag (-3,0)(-1,-2)(1,2)(3,0); fuseball cargo = a single yellow 4-point cross (glyphs.ts 104-105); flipper cargo = no emblem (correct).
reasoning: The flipper-cargo 'no emblem' rule is right, so the concept is right and only the two emblem shapes are wrong. The fuse tanker's emblem in particular should be a blue/red/green/yellow bar, not one yellow stroke. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 624-650 (CM=2/CD=1 in effect). TANKP's chain (blank move to (-5,-2) then draw to (-3,6),(0,-6),(3,6),(5,-2)) decodes exactly as the claim states — a genuine zigzag/W spanning the body, CSTAT TURQOI confirmed verbatim at line 626. TANTAB three-way pick confirmed verbatim at ALDISP.MAC 696. Attacked the TANKF decode by hand-tracing the running OLX/OLZ: BLUE draws (0,0)->(-0xC,0); blank to (0,0xC); RED draws (0,0xC)->(0,0) [a full-length line back to origin]; GREEN draws (0,0)->(0,-0xC); blank back to (0,0); YELLOW draws (0,0)->(0xC,0). Net shape is a genuine 4-armed plus/cross (BLUE left, RED top, GREEN bottom, YELLOW right, each arm length 0xC from center), not '3 lines + 1 dot' as the claim's prose states for the RED element ('CSTAT RED dot at (0,0C)') — 'SCVEC 0,0,CB' is not a zero-length vector like the ROM's genuine dot idiom (VCTR 0,0,CB used elsewhere, e.g. ESHOT), it computes a real delta from the current position. This does not change the substance of the divergence (four ROM colours vs our single yellow emblem) but the per-segment shape claim needs correcting. [CORRECTION] The RED segment of the fuse-tanker emblem is not a stand-alone dot — it is a real 0xC-unit line from (0,0xC) down to the origin, the top arm of a symmetric 4-colour plus/cross (BLUE left, RED top, GREEN bottom, YELLOW right), not '3 lines + 1 dot'.

title: The spiker (Theurer's 'traler') is a GREEN 21-point authored spiral in 4 hand-drawn phases; ours is an ORANGE 12-point procedural spiral rotated 90 degrees per frame

ours.verbatim: return [{ points: rotPoints(SPIKER_BASE, a), closed: false, color: 'orange' }]

claim: SPIRA1-4 (ALVROM.MAC 516-620; PICLO entry `PITAB SPIRA1,PTSPI1 ;SPIRALS`, ALVROM.MAC 2110) are FOUR separately-authored 21-vertex spirals, each opening `CSTAT GREEN`, CM=2 CD=1. SPIRA1's absolute vertices (hex object units) are (1,-1) (0,-2) (-2,-2) (-4,0) (-4,4) (0,6) (5,5) (8,0) (7,-7) (0,-0A) (-8,-8) (-0C,0) (-9,9) (0,0E) (0B,0B) (10,0) (0C,-0C) (0,-12) (-0E,-0E) (-14,0) (-0F,0F) — an outward-winding spiral of steadily growing radius. SPIRA2/3/4 are the same spiral advanced one quarter-turn but re-authored, not rotated. Ours is 12 points on r = 2 + 0.7i at 60-degree steps, coloured ORANGE, with the 4 frames produced by rotating that one curve by 90 degrees (glyphs.ts 112-121). ALCOMN.MAC 369 `TRACOL=GREEN` fixes the colour.
reasoning: The colour is flatly wrong — orange is not in the pulled palette at all (the ROM's eight slots are white/yellow/purple/red/turquoise/green/blue/blue) — and the shape has 12 vertices where the ROM has 21, with a different winding rate. Both are visible from across the room. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 516-620 (CM=2/CD=1 in effect, CSTAT GREEN confirmed verbatim at line 522; TRACOL=GREEN confirmed at ALCOMN.MAC 369; PITAB SPIRA1,PTSPI1 confirmed verbatim at line 2110). Hand-traced SPIRA1's 21 absolute vertices: radius grows monotonically from ~1.4 to ~21.2 units across ~2.6 revolutions at 45-degree angular steps, confirming 'outward-winding spiral of steadily growing radius' and the 21-vertex count exactly as transcribed. Colour palette check (ALCOMN.MAC 351-358: WHITE=0,YELLOW=1,PURPLE=2,RED=3,TURQOI=4,GREEN=5,BLUE=6,BLULET=7) confirms ORANGE is genuinely absent from the ROM's 8 slots — our spikerGlyph() uses 'orange', a flat colour error. Our SPIKER_BASE has 12 vertices at 60-degree steps (2 full turns, radius 2 to 9.7) vs the ROM's 21 vertices at 45-degree steps (2.6 turns, radius 1.4 to 21.2) — real vertex-count and winding-rate divergence. However, I attacked the specific sub-claim that SPIRA2/3/4 are 're-authored, not rotated': rotating every one of SPIRA1's 21 (x,y) pairs by exactly 90 degrees CCW reproduces SPIRA2's table point-for-point (verified all 21); 180 degrees reproduces SPIRA3; 270 degrees (90 CW) reproduces SPIRA4. They are precise mathematical rotations of one base spiral, just hardcoded as 4 separate tables rather than computed at runtime — the opposite of what the claim's prose asserts. This is a real error in the finding's own reasoning (and ironically means our code's rotate-one-table approach is architecturally identical to what the ROM's data encodes), but it does not rescue the actual colour and vertex-count divergence, which are independently verified and real. [CORRECTION] SPIRA2/SPIRA3/SPIRA4 are NOT independently re-authored data distinct from a rotation — they are exact 90/180/270-degree rotations of SPIRA1 (verified point-by-point). The claim's 'advanced one quarter-turn but re-authored, not rotated' is backwards; only the colour (orange vs GREEN) and vertex-count/winding-rate (12 @ 60deg vs 21 @ 45deg) divergences should be relied on.

title: Enemy shot: colours (white hooks + red dots) are right, the hook geometry and the frame source are not

ours.verbatim: strokes.push({ points: [corner, hook], closed: false, color: 'white' })

claim: ESHOT1 (MESHO1, ALVROM.MAC 700-721, CM=1) is FOUR short WHITE segments on the diagonals — (-11,11)->(-17,17), (-17,-11)->(-11,-17), (17,-17)->(11,-11), (11,17)->(17,11) (decimal) — plus FOUR RED dots at (+/-6,+/-6) (hex 6). Each of ESHOT2/3/4 rotates the same idea with its own coordinates. Ours builds the four white hooks on the CARDINAL axes at radius BOLT_SIZE=10 with a perpendicular 6-unit hook, and the four red dots on the cardinals at radius 4.5 (glyphs.ts 203-227): a pinwheel, not four outward diagonal ticks. Frame selection also differs: the ROM picks the ESHOT frame off the global frame counter (`LDA QFRAME / ASL / AND I,6 / ADC I,PTESHO`, ALDISP.MAC 910-914), we pick it off the bullet's DEPTH (`enemyBoltGlyph(Math.floor(b.depth * 8))`, render.ts 297). Colours match: ICHCOL=WHITE (ALCOMN.MAC 361) and the inner CSTAT RED (ALVROM.MAC 712).
reasoning: The two-colour composition (white outer ticks, red inner dots) is authentic; the arms sit 45 degrees off and the animation is keyed to travel instead of time, so a stationary bolt would never spin. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 700-721 (MESHO1 macro, CM=1/CD=1 so unscaled; citation line 704 'SCVEC -17.,17.,CB' verbatim-confirmed). Hand-traced OLX/OLZ through the macro: the four drawn WHITE segments are exactly (-11,11)->(-17,17), (-17,-11)->(-11,-17), (17,-17)->(11,-11), (11,17)->(17,11) as claimed (decimal, correctly distinguishing the '.'-suffixed decimal literals from the file's default hex radix), then four genuine RED dots (real 'VCTR 0,0,CB' zero-vectors, unlike the fuse-tanker's fake 'dot' in V-007) at (+/-6,+/-6). These four ROM segments sit on the 45/135/225/315-degree diagonals. Our enemyBoltGlyph (glyphs.ts:205-227, cited stroke.push at line 215 confirmed verbatim) builds its 4 hooks at k*90 degrees (0/90/180/270, the cardinals) with a perpendicular 6-unit tick — genuinely a 45-degree rotational offset from the ROM, i.e. a pinwheel on the wrong axes. Frame-source check: ALDISP.MAC 910-914 (LDA QFRAME/ASL/AND I,6/ADC I,PTESHO, all confirmed verbatim at those exact lines) computes index = (QFRAME*2) AND 6 added to a word-pointer table, i.e. selects one of 4 ESHOT tables by QFRAME AND 3 — a true global-frame-counter selection. render.ts:297 confirmed passing 'Math.floor(b.depth*8)' into enemyBoltGlyph, i.e. depth-driven, not frame-driven. Both halves of the claim hold under macro decoding and governing-code checks.

title: Player charge (bullet) is drawn as two stroked octagons; the ROM's DIARA2 is 17 DOTS in two rings
claim: DIARA2 (ALVROM.MAC 379-403, CM=1) is built entirely from SCDOT (blank move + `VCTR 0,0,CB` — a single lit point; macro at ALVROM.MAC 89-92). Inner ring, CSTAT PSHCTR: dots at (0,0) (7,0) (5,5) (0,7) (-5,5) (-7,0) (-5,-5) (0,-7) (5,-5). Outer ring, CSTAT YELLOW: dots at (0F,0) (0B,0B) (0,0F) (-0B,0B) (-0B,0) (-0B,-0B) (0,-0B) (0B,-0B) — note this outer ring is deliberately IRREGULAR: the -x and -y cardinal dots sit at 0B (11), not 0F (15). Seventeen dots, no lines, two different colours. playerBulletGlyph() returns two CLOSED STROKED octagon outlines of radius 3 and 6, both 'white' (glyphs.ts 273-285), which render.ts then recolours wholesale.
reasoning: A dotted diamond-of-dots and a pair of stroked octagons look nothing alike at the sizes a charge is drawn. The 7:15 radius ratio and the two-colour split (centre vs rim) are also lost. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 383-401 (CM=1/CD=1 in effect; citation line 385 'SCDOT 0,0' verbatim-confirmed as DIARA2's first dot). SCDOT expands to a blank SCVEC move plus a genuine zero-length VCTR (a real dot, no connecting lines anywhere in this picture). Counted the inner ring (CSTAT PSHCTR): 9 SCDOT lines including a centre dot at (0,0) plus 8 around radius 7, matching the claim's list exactly. Counted the outer ring (CSTAT YELLOW, confirmed verbatim at line 394): 8 SCDOT lines at (0xF,0),(0xB,0xB),(0,0xF),(-0xB,0xB),(-0xB,0),(-0xB,-0xB),(0,-0xB),(0xB,-0xB) — verified the claimed asymmetry directly: +x/+y cardinals sit at radius 0xF(15) while -x/-y cardinals sit at radius 0xB(11), with all four diagonals uniformly at radius 11 (11,11 etc.) — a real, deliberate irregularity in the source data, not a transcription artifact. Total 9+8=17 dots exactly, two colours (PSHCTR-indexed and fixed YELLOW), zero connecting lines. Our playerBulletGlyph (glyphs.ts:280-285, citation octagon(3) at line 282 confirmed) is two closed stroked octagon outlines, both white — categorically different primitive (strokes vs dots), different topology (rings of lines vs rings of points), one fewer colour axis, and no radius asymmetry. Holds.

title: The CHACOU ammo tint recolours the whole bullet; the ROM recolours only the charge's CENTRE dots, and the low tint is BLUE

source.verbatim: STY COLPOR+PSHCTR	;SET UP COLOR FOR CENTER OF PLAYER SOT

ours.verbatim: strokeGlyph(ctx, playerBulletGlyph(), p.x, p.y, 0.45 + b.depth * 0.35, renderTime * 5, 14, tint)

claim: DSPCHG (ALDISP.MAC 919-930) picks ZYELLO ('PLENTY'), ZBLUE ('LOW', when CHACOU >= NPCHARG-2 = 6) or ZRED ('OUT', when CHACOU >= NPCHARG = 8; NPCHARG=8 at ALCOMN.MAC 812) and stores it into `COLPOR+PSHCTR` — the colour-RAM slot used ONLY by DIARA2's inner ring (`CSTAT PSHCTR`, ALVROM.MAC 384). The outer ring stays `CSTAT YELLOW` (ALVROM.MAC 394) at all times. Our thresholds are right (>=8 red, >=6 cyan, else yellow — glyphs.ts 292-296) but strokeGlyph's `override` recolours EVERY sub-stroke of the glyph (render.ts 83), so both rings change; and the 6-7 tint is 'cyan' (turquoise), where the ROM uses ZBLUE.
reasoning: The ROM's tell is a hot CORE inside a permanently-yellow shell; ours is a wholly-recoloured blob. The blue-vs-turquoise slip is small but is a palette error (GlyphColor has no 'blue' — the type needs one). [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALDISP.MAC 919-930: LDY I,ZYELLO / CMP CHACOU,NPCHARG-2 / IFCS LDY ZBLUE / CMP NPCHARG / IFCS LDY ZRED / STY COLPOR+PSHCTR, all lines confirmed verbatim at 920-930 (citation line 930 exact). NPCHARG=8 confirmed verbatim at ALCOMN.MAC 812, so the thresholds are >=6 (NPCHARG-2) for the ZBLUE 'low' tint and >=8 for the ZRED 'out' tint, matching our playerBulletColor() thresholds exactly (glyphs.ts:292-296). PSHCTR=8 (ALCOMN.MAC 383, 'PLAYER SOT CENTER') is a colour-RAM slot index, and DIARA2's 'CSTAT PSHCTR' (ALVROM.MAC 384) is used only for the inner 9-dot ring before the code explicitly switches to a fixed 'CSTAT YELLOW' (line 394) for the outer 8-dot ring — so the ROM genuinely recolours only the centre, never the rim. Checked render.ts:285 and the strokeGlyph implementation (render.ts:72-99): the 'override' parameter is applied uniformly inside the stroke loop ('GLYPH_HEX[override ?? stroke.color]') with no distinction between the two octagon sub-strokes, so passing 'tint' there genuinely recolours both rings, confirming the claim. The blue-vs-cyan slip is also directly corroborated by our own code's comment at glyphs.ts:288-291 acknowledging 'the palette's blue, GlyphColor has no separate blue' — an admitted type/palette gap, not a stretch by the auditor. Holds on all counts: governing-code check, macro/colour-RAM decode, and threshold arithmetic.

title: Enemy-death explosion is drawn yellow; the ROM's EXPL1-4 are WHITE
claim: EXPL1..EXPL4 (ALVROM.MAC 344-376) each open `CSTAT WHITE` and emit SPOK16 (the 16-spoke macro, ALVROM.MAC 120-145) at CM = 1, 2, 4, 8 — i.e. 16 spokes doubling in radius across four frames. DSPEXP confirms the colour at runtime: `LDY I,EXPCOL` (ALDISP.MAC 936) with `EXPCOL=WHITE` (ALCOMN.MAC 366). Our burst is 16 spokes with a scale ladder 1/2/4/8 and a 7->14 brightness ramp (correct), but strokes them in '#ffe66b' — a warm yellow.
reasoning: The 16-spoke geometry and the doubling scale ladder ARE the ROM's; only the colour is invented. A one-constant fix. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:344-376. EXPL1-4 are exactly as claimed: each opens `CSTAT WHITE` (350/358/366/374) then calls SPOK16 (macro at 120-145, verified: ICVEC + 16 SCVEC pairs = 16 spokes) at CM=1,2,4,8 (345-372) with CB stepping 07->0E (347/356/364/372, i.e. brightness 7->14, matching the claim's '7->14 brightness ramp'). EXPCOL=WHITE is confirmed at ALCOMN.MAC:366, and ALCOMN is a shipped .INCLUDE, so the DSPEXP citation is legitimate (not decoy-tainted). Checked our side is governing: render.ts drawEnemyBurst (460-477) is invoked from drawExplosions only for ex.kind==='enemy', and fx.ts:185-186 spawns that burst specifically on the 'enemy-death' event -- this is the actual code path, not overridden elsewhere. ENEMY_BURST_COLOR = '#ffe66b' at render.ts:460 is unambiguously yellow, not white. No unit/radix trick rescues this -- WHITE=0 is a color-RAM index, not a numeric quantity that could be misread. Holds cleanly.

title: Player splat: the ROM SPLAT is one 26-vertex jagged outline whose colour switches every two vertices; ours is two procedural concentric star rings in one colour per frame

ours.verbatim: jaggedStarPath(ctx, ex.x, ex.y, ex.spokes, ex.radius, ex.radius * 0.45)

claim: SPLAT (ALVROM.MAC 806-850, CM=2 CD=1 CB=7) is a SINGLE closed jagged outline of 26 absolute vertices — (18,-8) (38,8) (20,0C) (24,14) (1C,15) (22,20) (10,16) (12,30) (4,28) (-0A,2D) (-0C,12) (-2E,18) (-1C,0) (-26,-8) (-20,-0A) (-26,-17) (-0D,-0E) (-10,-22) (-0C,-20) (-8,-2C) (4,-20) (10,-2C) (12,-18) (22,-1E) (18,-8) (0,0) — with the colour re-stated every TWO vertices, cycling PDIWHI -> PDIRED -> PDIYEL -> PDIWHI ... (indices 9/11/10, ALVROM.MAC 812/816/819 and ALCOMN.MAC 384-386), so all three colours are on screen SIMULTANEOUSLY around the ring. SPLAT1-6 (ALVROM.MAC 793-804) then re-draw that one picture at six SCAL sizes. Ours draws two concentric procedural jagged rings (outer/inner radius alternating) in ONE colour per frame, cycling white->red->yellow across frames (render.ts 495-507).
reasoning: The ROM's death splat is a single ragged multicoloured tear, not a pair of clean star rings; the colour cycle is spatial (per vertex-pair) not temporal (per frame). This is the player's death animation, seen every life lost. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:806-850. Traced the full SPLAT chain vertex-by-vertex: ICVEC establishes origin, then exactly 26 absolute-vertex entries (SCVEC 18,-8,0 through the closing SCVEC 18,-8,CB and final SCVEC 0,0,0) matching the finding's list VERBATIM, coordinate for coordinate. Color re-statement is real: CSTAT PDIWHI/PDIRED/PDIYEL appear every two SCVECs (812/816/819/822/825/828/831/834/837/840/843/846), and PDIWHI=9, PDIRED=11, PDIYEL=10 are confirmed at ALCOMN.MAC:384-386 (a shipped .INCLUDE, legitimate citation) exactly as cited. One minor irregularity the finding doesn't flag: the cycle isn't perfectly WHI->RED->YEL->WHI throughout -- at one point (lines 837-839) it goes RED->WHITE->YEL, skipping a beat -- but this doesn't change the substance: it's still one closed multi-vertex outline with color re-stated every ~2 vertices, all colors present simultaneously around the ring. Our side verified as governing: drawPlayerSplat (render.ts:495-507) draws two calls to jaggedStarPath, ex.color is a single string applied to both ctx.stroke() calls in one frame, and fx.ts:92 confirms SPLAT_CYCLE cycles per spawned splat/frame count, not per vertex. The claim that ours is temporal (per-frame) cycling of ONE color vs the ROM's spatial (per-vertex-pair) SIMULTANEOUS tri-color survives full scrutiny.

title: Fuseball: the ROM's FUSE0-3 are 5-colour authored scribbles (red/yellow/green/purple/turquoise); ours is a 9-leg procedural starburst in 3 colours
claim: FUSE0..FUSE3 (ALVROM.MAC 950-1095, CM=2 CD=1 CB=7) are four authored frames, each composed of FIVE colour groups in a fixed order — CSTAT RED, CSTAT YELLOW, CSTAT GREEN, CSTAT PURPLE, CSTAT TURQOI (ALVROM.MAC 955/961/968/975/983) — every group an open polyline of 5-7 absolute vertices. FUSE0's red arm alone is (-4,6) (1,0C) (-5,0E) (1,12) (-1,18); its purple arm is (-4,-1A)m (-4,-14) (-0A,-14) (-7,-0D) (-9,-6) (-3,-8) (0,0); and so on — roughly 29 vertices per frame in 5 colours. Ours generates 9 radial legs from the origin with sinusoidally-writhing angles/lengths, cycling only red/yellow/cyan (glyphs.ts 141-157). GREEN and PURPLE never appear on our fuseball.
reasoning: The fuseball is the ROM's most colourful sprite and its 5-colour scribble is its signature; a 3-colour procedural starburst reads as a different creature. The four frames are also authored (not one shape re-parameterised). [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:950-1094. Verified FUSE0's five CSTAT groups in order RED(955)/YELLOW(961)/GREEN(968)/PURPLE(975)/TURQOI(983), matching the claim's citation exactly (line 975 verbatim 'CSTAT PURPLE' confirmed). Checked FUSE0's red arm vertices (-4,6)(1,0C)(-5,0E)(1,12)(-1,18) and purple arm (-4,-1A)(-4,-14)(-0A,-14)(-7,-0D)(-9,-6)(-3,-8)(0,0) against source lines 956-960 and 976-982 -- both match verbatim. FUSE1/2/3 (990-1094) confirmed to repeat the same 5-color-group structure with different geometry per frame -- these are authored, not one shape re-parameterized, as claimed. Our side (glyphs.ts:141-157) confirmed: FUSE_COLORS = ['red','yellow','cyan'] (3 colors, no green/purple), FUSE_LEGS=9 procedural radial legs with sinusoidal writhe from the origin -- structurally nothing like the ROM's 5 fixed authored polylines per frame. No unit trick applies (color names, not scaled numerics). Holds.

title: Starfield: the ROM's four star pictures are ~22 dots each at authored coordinates; ours are 5 dots each at invented unit directions
claim: MSTAR1-MSTAR4 (ALVROM.MAC 405-515, CM=4 CD=1) are four macros of 20-22 SCDOTs each at authored absolute coordinates. MSTAR1's 22 dots (hex): (-8,0) (8,0C) (10,0) (8,30) (-30,20) (-34,-20) (-8,-48) (48,-20) (44,28) (18,50) (-38,44) (-48,-8) (-40,-50) (10,-70) (58,-50) (68,-8) (58,50) (8,70) (-40,68) (-78,28) (-70,-28) (-70,-68). Ours reuses four 5-dot 'pictures' of hand-picked unit vectors scattered radially from screen centre (render.ts 133-138). Count (5 vs 22) and every coordinate differ. The colour IS right for early waves: `LDA I,BLUE ;BLUE STARS IN WAVES 1-4` (ALDISP.MAC 2952) vs STAR_COLOR '#7fc3ff' (render.ts 129) — though from wave 5 the ROM varies the star colour per plane (ALDISP.MAC 2949-2960), which we never do.
reasoning: The '4 reused star pictures' structure is right; the pictures themselves are not, and at 5 dots the warp field is a quarter as dense as the arcade's. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:405-504. Counted MSTAR1's SCDOTs: 22 entries from (-8,0) through (-70,-68), matching the finding's list vertex-for-vertex including the cited line 409 'SCDOT 8,0C' (the second dot). MSTAR2/3/4 also verified at 20 SCDOTs each (a minor undercount by the finding's '20-22' range, which is itself accurate -- MSTAR1 is 22, the other three are 20). Checked the color claim against ALDISP.MAC:2949-2960 (DSTARF, a shipped routine): confirmed CURWAV<5 forces LDA I,BLUE (line 2952, 'BLUE STARS IN WAVES 1-4'), while CURWAV>=5 computes color from the plane index AND 7 (with index 7 remapped to 4) -- i.e. genuine per-plane color variance from wave 5 on, exactly as claimed. Our side (render.ts:129-138) confirmed: STAR_PICTURE_DOTS is four 5-element arrays of unit-vector directions (nothing like the ROM's 20-22 authored absolute coordinates), and STAR_COLOR is a single constant '#7fc3ff' used unconditionally in drawStarfield with no wave-dependent branch anywhere in the function. Both the density (5 vs ~20-22) and the missing wave>=5 color variance survive verification.

title: The lives icon is a hand-drawn chevron; the ROM's LIFE1 is the claw silhouette itself

ours.verbatim: ctx.moveTo(lx, baseY); ctx.lineTo(cx, apexY); ctx.lineTo(rx, baseY)

claim: LIFE1 (ALVROM.MAC 165-185, CM=6 CD=1 CB=6) opens `CSTAT YELLOW` then draws the closed absolute chain (0,0) (4,-2) (1,-3) (3,-2) (0,-1) (-3,-2) (-1,-3) (-4,-2) (0,0) — the claw's own W-shaped silhouette, scaled x6, with LIFE0 adding the 0A blank advance between icons. drawClawIcon (render.ts 521-549) instead strokes a two-leg chevron plus a horizontal cross-brace and a white apex dot, sized off `size`. Colour is right (CLAW_COLOR '#ffe600' vs CSTAT YELLOW).
reasoning: We already own the correct 8-vertex claw shape (CLAW_DELTAS, V-002) — the lives HUD just doesn't use it. Cheap fidelity win. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:165-186. LIFE1's chain (0,0)(4,-2)(1,-3)(3,-2)(0,-1)(-3,-2)(-1,-3)(-4,-2)(0,0) matches source verbatim (lines 173-181), and the CSTAT YELLOW at 172 plus LIFE0's blank SCVEC 0A,0,0 at 182 are both confirmed. Traced LSYMBL/LSYMB0 usage to ALSCOR.MAC:186 ('LDA LSYMBL ;DEFAULT LIFE PIC') inside the lives-counter display loop (175-197, iterating up to 6 icons via SCOBUF) -- this really is the lives-HUD picture, not a self-test-only asset. Our drawClawIcon (render.ts:521-549) draws a two-leg chevron + horizontal cross-brace + white apex dot -- structurally nothing like the ROM's closed 8-vertex zigzag. Divergence holds. HOWEVER the finding's specific recommendation is built on a false premise that needs flagging: CLAW_DELTAS (glyphs.ts:247-256, cited as V-002) is NOT the LIFE1 picture -- it's a byte-exact transcription of NCRS1-8, the 'DISPLAY CURSOR' in-flight rotating-claw graphics, which I found still shipped at ALDISP.MAC:1917-1997 (verified NCRS1's deltas 0,-2/2,-1/3,4/-3,-3/-1,0/0,2/2,1/-3,-1 match CLAW_DELTAS[0] exactly). NCRS1-8 and LIFE1 are two distinct ROM pictures for two distinct purposes (rotating gameplay cursor vs static lives icon); their vertex chains differ (LIFE1 is a symmetric W, NCRS1-8 are asymmetric roll frames). So 'we already own the correct shape, just reuse it' is wrong -- a true fix needs new authored vertex data matching LIFE1's own chain, not a call into playerClawGlyph(). The core divergence claim stands; the 'cheap, just reuse V-002' framing does not. [CORRECTION] CLAW_DELTAS/V-002 (NCRS1-8) is a different ROM picture from LIFE1 (confirmed shipped separately at ALDISP.MAC:1917-1997, used for the in-flight rotating cursor, not the lives HUD). Reusing it verbatim will not reproduce LIFE1's actual 8-vertex chain -- new vertex data must be authored from ALVROM.MAC:173-181 for a true fix; the size should not be called 's' on the assumption existing data can be repointed.

title: The TEMPEST logo is its own stroke alphabet laid out in a stair-step, not the message font on a straight baseline

ours.verbatim: drawGlowText(ctx, 'TEMPEST', W / 2, titleY, size, pass.color, 8 + Math.round(20 * pass.depth))

claim: TEMLIT (ALVROM.MAC 1297-1351, CM=1 CD=1 CB=6) has SEVEN dedicated logo-letter routines with their own geometry — e.g. `E: VCTR -50,0,CB / VCTR -14,-40,CB / VCTR 70,0,CB / VCTR -70,0,0 / VCTR -14,-40,CB / VCTR 84,0,CB` (a BACK-SLANTED E, its arms stepping left as they descend), and `M`, `P`, `S`, `T` likewise (ALVROM.MAC 1318-1351) — none of which is the ANVGAN message glyph for that letter. The layout is NOT a straight baseline: after CNTR it moves (-1B0,100), draws T, +60 E, +24 M, +34 P, then JUMPS (0F8,48) — up and right — draws E, +16,+28 S, then +60,-60 T. So 'TEMP' sits low-left and 'EST' climbs up and right in a stair-step. We render the string 'TEMPEST' through the ordinary message font (render.ts 679 -> vecText -> layoutText) on one horizontal baseline.
reasoning: The logo is the first thing the cabinet shows. Its letterforms and its diagonal stagger are unique to TEMLIT and are simply absent from ours — we draw HUD text, blown up. (The rainbow depth-stack around it is a separate, reasonable interpretation.) [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC:1297-1351, well clear of the dead .IF NE,SPACG block that starts at 1359. Verified the entire TEMLIT move/letter sequence verbatim: CNTR, VCTR -1B0,100,0, JSRL T, VCTR 60,0,0, JSRL E, VCTR 24,0,0, JSRL M, VCTR 34,0,0, JSRL P, VCTR 0F8,48,0 (line 1312, the cited verbatim, confirmed), JSRL E, VCTR 16,28,0, JSRL S, VCTR 60,-60,0, JMPL T -- an exact match. Verified E's letterform (1322-1328) matches the finding's quoted strokes exactly. Cross-checked ANVGAN.MAC's message font: CHAR.E (line 74: VCTR 0,24,.BRITE...) and CHAR.T (line 192: VCTR 8,0,0...) are structurally unrelated stroke sequences to TEMLIT's E/T -- confirms TEMLIT really is its own alphabet, not the message font reused. Traced the actual pen deltas through T/E/M/P: the explicit inter-letter jump before the second E (0F8,48 = dx+248,dy+72) is unambiguously up-and-right in this source's Y-up convention (confirmed by T's own first stroke VCTR 0,80,CB drawing its vertical stem upward from baseline), so the 'stair-step, not a straight baseline' claim is real. Two things to flag: (1) the finding says 'SEVEN dedicated logo-letter routines' but only FIVE unique routines exist (T, E, M, P, S at 1318/1322/1329/1338/1344) -- T and E are each reused once to spell the 7-letter word TEMPEST; 'seven routines' should read 'five routines called seven times'. (2) 'EST climbs up and right' is only true through the S -- the final jump before the last T (VCTR 60,-60,0, dx+96/dy-96) reverses and drops back down, so the pen path is climb-climb-then-drop, not a monotonic ascent across all of EST. Neither correction changes the underlying verdict: our render.ts:679 does draw the plain shared-font 'TEMPEST' string via drawGlowText on one straight baseline, so the core divergence (own alphabet + staggered non-linear layout, entirely absent from ours) is real. [CORRECTION] The finding overcounts logo routines as 'seven dedicated' -- there are five (T, E, M, P, S), with T and E each reused once to spell the seven letters. Also, the climb is not monotonic: the final T's approach jump (VCTR 60,-60,0) moves down-right, reversing the preceding up-right climb through E and S.

title: The score/lives HUD uses the level-cycling colour and adds captions; the ROM template is GREEN score, YELLOW lives, GREEN hi-score, BLUE level, no captions
claim: The SCORES template (ALVROM.MAC 1955-2017) fixes the colour of every HUD field: `CSTAT GREEN` before the 6-digit player score (1958), `CSTAT YELLOW` before the row of LIFE0 life icons (1971-1977), `CSTAT GREEN` before the 6-digit high score (1979), `CSTAT BLUE` before the 2-digit LEVEL (1987-1989), `CSTAT GREEN` before the 3 hi-score initials (1990-1994). Ours draws score, level AND hi-score in the level-cycling `color` (render.ts 787/790/794) and adds three captions the ROM has no glyphs for — 'SCORE', 'LEVEL', 'HI-SCORE' (render.ts 788/791/795) — in a steel-blue that is not a palette colour. The ROM also places the high score directly under player 1's score with the level below it, not centred at the top.
reasoning: Every HUD number changing hue with the level is a modern flourish; the ROM's fixed green/yellow/blue coding is how the player reads the panel. The captions are pure invention. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALVROM.MAC 1955-1994: CSTAT GREEN before SCP1SC (score, 1958-1962), CSTAT YELLOW before SCP1LI (life icons, 1971-1972), CSTAT GREEN before SCHISC (hi-score, 1979-1980), CSTAT BLUE before SCLEVL (level, 1987-1988), CSTAT GREEN before SCHIIN (initials, 1990-1992) — the finding's colour-per-field table is verbatim correct, and the template only ever calls JSRL CHAR. / JSRL LIFE0 (digit and life-icon glyphs), never a text-drawing routine, so 'no glyphs for SCORE/LEVEL/HI-SCORE captions' holds. Checked our side is governing: render.ts:917 computes `color = LEVEL_COLORS[(s.level-1) % LEVEL_COLORS.length]` (the per-level cycling hue) and drawHud (called at line 1014 with that same `color`) uses it for the score number (787), level number (790) AND hi-score number (794) alike — confirmed by tracing the call chain, not just the isolated snippet. The three captions 'SCORE'/'LEVEL'/'HI-SCORE' at 788/791/795 use LABEL_COLOR = 'rgba(175,210,255,0.9)', a translucent steel-blue absent from the 8-colour arcade palette. No macro or unit trap applies (plain colour/text citations). Holds against every attack angle.

title: Per-level colour: the ROM swaps the ENTIRE 8-slot colour RAM every 16 waves, recolouring every enemy; we recolour only the well
claim: COLTAB (ALDISP.MAC 2405-2456) is SIX banks of 8 bytes; INICOL indexes it with the wave number (`LDA CURWAV / AND I,70 / ... / ORA I,07`, ALDISP.MAC 2353-2374) and writes each byte's low nibble to colour slots 0-7 and its high nibble to slots 8-15. So slot 3 (FLIPPERS) is ZRED in bank 1 but ZPURPL in bank 2 and ZGREEN in bank 4; slot 6 (WELL, `.BYTE ZBLUE ;WELL(6)`, ALDISP.MAC 2412) is ZBLUE in bank 1, ZGREEN in bank 2, ZTURQOI in bank 3... The whole cast changes colour together, wave group by wave group. Our LEVEL_COLORS (render.ts 19-22) is an 8-hue list used ONLY for the well/HUD (render.ts 917, 941-944); every enemy keeps a hard-coded GLYPH_HEX hue for the whole game (render.ts 38-46).
reasoning: This is why the arcade's later waves look like different games. It is also the deepest change on this list: the renderer would need a palette-slot indirection (enemy -> slot -> per-wave bank) instead of enemy -> fixed hex. The title was corrected during coverage review from "per wave" to "every 16 waves": COLTAB advances by wave-GROUP (CURWAV AND 0x70, masking bits 4-6), not on every individual wave. A refuter reading the old "per wave" title could have dismissed or killed this otherwise-valid finding on a false premise, since the substantive claim (the ROM recolours the entire cast wave-group by wave-group, we recolour only the well) is correct and stands unchanged. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALDISP.MAC 2353-2456 (INICOL + COLTAB); confirmed both are ordinary code in a linked module (ALDISP appears in ALEXEC.MAP, not in the decoy list) with no .IF NE,SPACG gate anywhere near this block (the SPACG-conditional blocks are all in ALVROM.MAC, a different file). Decoded the macro: LDA CURWAV/AND I,70 masks bits 4-6 (a hex file — 5F/70/07 aren't valid octal), grouping CURWAV into buckets of 16 (matches the corrected 'every 16 waves' title, not 'per wave'); CMP I,5F/IFCS clamps; LSR halves; ORA I,07 forces the low 3 bits, landing X on the last byte of an 8-byte bank; the BEGIN/MIEND loop decrements X and Y together, so COLRAM[k] = low-nibble(COLTAB[bank_base+k]) and COLRAM[8+k] = high-nibble(COLTAB[bank_base+k]) for k=0..7 exactly as claimed — six 8-byte banks (base 0/8/16/24/32/40), confirmed by counting non-comment .BYTE lines. Checked our side: LEVEL_COLORS (render.ts 19-22) feeds only `wellColor`/`color` into drawTube (944) — the well — while flipperGlyph/tankerGlyph/etc. (glyphs.ts) hard-code a fixed GlyphColor ('red', 'purple', ...) with no level/wave parameter at all, so the claim that enemies never recolour is verified in the glyph library itself, not just render.ts's call site. The core claim (ROM recolours the whole cast wave-group by wave-group via a bank swap; we recolour only the well) survives every attack. CORRECTION: the finding's worked example for slot 6 (WELL) misidentifies two of its three cross-bank values. Recomputing bank_base+6 for each bank: bank1=ZBLUE (2412, correct), bank2=ZRED (line 2420, NOT ZGREEN as claimed — ZGREEN is bank2 offset 1, line 2415), bank3=ZYELLO (line 2428, NOT ZTURQOI as claimed — ZTURQOI is actually bank4's WELL colour, line 2436). The slot-3 (FLIPPERS) example in the same claim (ZRED bank1, ZPURPL bank2, ZGREEN bank4) IS correct on recount. The directional claim and the six-bank/nibble-split mechanism are right; only the specific WELL colour-per-bank illustration is wrong for 2 of 3 cited banks. [CORRECTION] WELL (colour slot 6) is ZBLUE in bank 1 (correct as cited) but ZRED in bank 2 (not ZGREEN) and ZYELLO in bank 3 (not ZTURQOI); ZTURQOI is actually bank 4's WELL colour. The FLIPPERS/slot-3 example elsewhere in the same claim is accurate and does not need correction.

title: SPARK1/SPARK2 — the yellow 4-dot sparkle a SHATTERED spike tip shows
claim: SPARK1/SPARK2 (ALVROM.MAC 670-697) are two YELLOW pictures of four dots each — SPARK1 on the axes at (+/-10,0),(0,+/-10) hex, SPARK2 on the diagonals at (+/-10,+/-10) — alternated to twinkle. They are hung off PTSPAR and used in two places: the SHATTERED enemy-line tip picks one at random (`LDA RANDOM / AND I,2 / ADC I,PTSPAR`, ALDISP.MAC 3193-3197) and the invader-player collision database references PTSPAR (ALDISP.MAC 982). We draw nothing for a shattered spike tip — drawSpikes always caps with the un-shattered white dot (render.ts 263-266).
reasoning: A shot spike tip in the arcade SPARKLES until it settles; ours never distinguishes shattered from intact. Small, self-contained addition on top of the existing white-dot cap.

title: SHRAP — the shrapnel picture (five yellow debris chunks plus four dots)
claim: SHRAP (ALVROM.MAC 876-947, CM=4 CD=1 CB=7) draws five separately-scaled CSTAT YELLOW debris pieces (piece 1: (25,-0E) (25,-13) (3A,-7) (2E,-6) (30,-8) (20,-0E); piece 2: 3 vertices; piece 3: 6; piece 4: 6; piece 5: 8) interleaved with JADOT dots at (10,6), (10,30), (-0C,10), (-2C,18), (-0C,-0C), (18,-24) — a blown-apart claw. We have no shrapnel shape; a generic particle spray (drawParticles, render.ts 444-454) stands in.
reasoning: Our particle spray already conveys 'the claw came apart'; the ROM's authored chunks are a nicety, and reproducing them well means matching the JSRL SCALE per-piece scaling too.

title: FUSEX1-3 — the floating WHITE score pop-ups '750' / '500' / '250'
claim: FUSEX1/2/3 (ALVROM.MAC 1096-1114) are not explosions at all — each sets CSTAT WHITE, SCAL 1,20, backs up `VCTR -36.,0,0` and then JSRLs digit glyphs: FUSEX1 = CHAR.7, CHAR.5, CHAR.0 ('750'); FUSEX2 = CHAR.5, CHAR.0, CHAR.0 ('500'); FUSEX3 = CHAR.2, CHAR.5, CHAR.0 ('250'). They are the score numbers that bloom where a fuseball dies (PICLO entry `PITAB FUSEX1,PTFUSX ;FUSE EXPLOSION`, ALVROM.MAC 2148). We render no floating score text anywhere.
reasoning: A visible, missing piece of feedback: the arcade tells you what a kill was worth, at the kill. Cheap to add — we already have the vector font and a per-explosion position.

title: SPLFU1-7 — the 7-stage star-burst ladder for a fuseball killing the player
claim: SPLFU1..SPLFU7 (ALVROM.MAC 852-874) re-use the star picture STAR1B at seven shrinking SCAL steps with a colour ladder: WHITE (SCAL 5,0), WHITE (4,60), YELLOW (4,40), YELLOW (4,20), RED (4,0), RED (3,60), BLUE (3,40). It is a distinct death animation, used when a FUSE (fuseball) takes the player — different from the SPLAT the other enemies trigger. We have exactly one player-death animation (drawPlayerSplat, render.ts 495-507).
reasoning: Distinct death animations per killer is a fidelity detail few will notice, and it needs a new fx kind plus a killer discriminant in the death event.

title: The DASH glyph is verbatim ROM data
claim: DASH (ALVROM.MAC 33-36) is `VCTR 0,12,0` / `VCTR 16,0,.BRITE` / `VCTR 8,-12,0` with `.BRITE=6` (ALVROM.MAC 22). The font this module re-exports (@arcade/shared/font) stores `'-': [[0, 12, 0], [16, 0, 1], [8, -12, 0]]` — the same three ops, same order, same blank/lit pattern, and the same 16x24 cell (CELL_W=16, CELL_H=24) and 24-unit advance convention.
reasoning: The one message-font glyph that lives in ALVROM (the rest are pulled in by `.INCLUDE ANVGAN`, ALVROM.MAC 26) matches exactly. NOTE: the remaining 40 glyphs could not be verified here — the glyph data lives in ANVGAN.MAC, which the audit rules place off-limits as a non-shipped module, so no per-letter finding can be cited. The cell geometry and the DASH routine are the only font facts ALVROM itself carries, and both agree.

title: The font has no HALF (1/2) and no COPYR (circle-C) glyph
claim: The ROM alphabet includes two composite glyphs beyond the letters/digits: HALF (ALVROM.MAC 30, 53-60) — `VCTR 16,24,.BRITE` then a SCAL 2 '1' over a '2', reached by typing a quote character — and COPYR (ALVROM.MAC 31, 37-52) — an outer circle of 8 VCTRs plus an inner C, reached by typing '#'. @arcade/shared/font ships neither; its GLYPH_CHARS is ' 0123456789ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ-,/_'. Every unsupported char degrades to a blank.
reasoning: The only ROM string that needs them is the copyright line (V-037), which we deliberately do not ship. There is no other use.

title: GAME OVER is GREEN
claim: The message table declares GAMOV as GREEN, scale 1, y=56h; the English literal is `EGAMOV: ASCVH <GAME OVER>` (ALLANG.MAC 97). We draw the same string in '#39ff14' (GLYPH_HEX green).
reasoning: Wording and colour both match the 1981 table.

title: PRESS START is RED
claim: PRESS is RED, scale 1, y=56h; English literal `EPRESS: ASCVH <PRESS START>` (ALLANG.MAC 111). We draw 'PRESS START' in '#ff2f4f' (GLYPH_HEX red), blinking, on the attract screen.
reasoning: Exact wording, exact colour.

title: HIGH SCORES is RED
claim: HIGHS is RED, and note SCALE 0 (the big cell) — the only message besides PLAYER and AVOID SPIKES to use it. English literal `EHIGHS: ASCVH 0BC,<HIGH SCORES>` (ALLANG.MAC 136), plural. We draw the plural 'HIGH SCORES' in '#ff2f4f'.
reasoning: Colour and the plural spelling both match. (Our size is a uniform 20px rather than the double-size scale-0 cell; that is a layout choice, not a palette or wording error.)

title: RATE YOURSELF is GREEN and the NOVICE/EXPERT ladder is RED
claim: RATE = GREEN (ALLANG.MAC 74, literal `ERATE: ASCVH <RATE YOURSELF>` at 146); NOVIC = RED and EXPER = RED (ALLANG.MAC 75-76). We draw 'RATE YOURSELF' green (#39ff14) and 'NOVICE'/'EXPERT' red (#ff2f4f, render.ts 708-709).
reasoning: The skill-select screen's three ROM strings all carry their ROM colours.

title: AVOID SPIKES is WHITE
claim: SPIKE is WHITE at scale 0 (double-size cell), y=0; English literal `ESPIKE: ASCVH -72.,<AVOID SPIKES>` (ALLANG.MAC 238). We draw 'AVOID SPIKES' in '#ffffff' during the warp hold.
reasoning: Wording and colour exact.

title: BONUS and TIME are both GREEN
claim: BONUS = GREEN scale 1 y=-70. (ALLANG.MAC 77) and TIME = GREEN scale 1 y=98h (ALLANG.MAC 78); literals `SBONUS: ASCVH 8B,<BONUS>` (164) and `ETIME: ASCVH 0E8,<TIME>` (166). We draw both banners in '#39ff14' on the level-clear warp (render.ts 993-994).
reasoning: Both between-wave banners match. (The numeric tallies beside them are still missing, but that is a gameplay gap, not a shape/colour one.)

title: SUPERZAPPER RECHARGE is drawn in BLULET (the blue letter slot)
claim: SUPZA uses BLULET (=7, the second letters slot, ALCOMN.MAC 352) — not the usual LETCOL green. English falls through to `FSUPZA: ASCVH <SUPERZAPPER RECHARGE>` (ALLANG.MAC 248-249), i.e. the English string IS 'SUPERZAPPER RECHARGE'. We draw exactly that string in '#1f8fff' blue.
reasoning: Exact wording and the correct (unusual) blue letter colour.

title: 'SPIN KNOB TO CHANGE' (TURQOISE) is replaced by 'SPIN OR ARROW KEYS TO CHANGE' in a non-palette steel-blue

ours.verbatim: drawGlowText(ctx, 'SPIN OR ARROW KEYS TO CHANGE', W / 2, H * 0.72, 16, 'rgba(150,190,255,0.7)', 6)

claim: PRMOV is TURQOI, scale 1, y=0; the English literal is `EPRMOV: ASCVH <SPIN KNOB TO CHANGE>` (ALLANG.MAC 126). Ours reads 'SPIN OR ARROW KEYS TO CHANGE' in 'rgba(150,190,255,0.7)' — a translucent steel-blue that is not one of the eight palette slots.
reasoning: The keyboard hint is a legitimate browser-port concession, but the colour is not: it should be the turquoise slot (GLYPH_HEX.cyan '#00e5ff'), at full opacity. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALLANG.MAC:70 `MESS PRMOV,TURQOI,1,0 ;SPIN` and :126 `EPRMOV: ASCVH <SPIN KNOB TO CHANGE>` — both verbatim as claimed; ALLANG is a linked module (ALEXEC.MAP), no SPACG gate applies to this text table. Decoded the MESS macro (defined at the top of ALLANG.MAC): field order is LIT,COL,SCA,YPO, so TURQOI is unambiguously the colour field, not a position or scale value — no macro trap. Checked our side: render.ts:710, inside drawSelect (the skill/level-select screen — same functional slot as the ROM's spin-to-change prompt, confirmed by the surrounding RATE YOURSELF/RANK/NOVICE/EXPERT text at 700-709 which maps 1:1 onto the ROM's skill-select screen), draws 'SPIN OR ARROW KEYS TO CHANGE' in 'rgba(150,190,255,0.7)' verbatim as cited — a translucent light blue distinct from GLYPH_HEX.cyan '#00e5ff' used elsewhere in this file for turquoise-coded elements. The keyboard-hint text substitution is conceded by the finding itself as a legitimate browser concession; only the colour is disputed. Holds.

title: 'PRESS FIRE TO SELECT' (YELLOW) is replaced by 'PRESS START / ENTER TO BEGIN' in the level-cycling colour

ours.verbatim: drawGlowText(ctx, 'PRESS START / ENTER TO BEGIN', W / 2, H * 0.72 + 32, 18, color, 12)

claim: PRFIR is YELLOW, scale 1, y=-10.; English literal `EPRFIR: ASCVH <PRESS FIRE TO SELECT>` (ALLANG.MAC 131). Ours reads 'PRESS START / ENTER TO BEGIN' in the per-level cycling `color` — so on level 1 it is blue, level 2 red, and so on.
reasoning: On the skill-select screen the ROM's confirm prompt is a fixed yellow 'PRESS FIRE TO SELECT'. Ours changes hue with the level and says something else. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALLANG.MAC:71 `MESS PRFIR,YELLOW,1,-10. ;PRESS FIRE` and :131 `EPRFIR: ASCVH <PRESS FIRE TO SELECT>` — both verbatim as claimed. Checked our side: render.ts:713, still inside drawSelect, draws 'PRESS START / ENTER TO BEGIN' using the `color` parameter — traced this parameter to its source: drawFrame (764-776) passes the same `color = LEVEL_COLORS[(s.level-1)%...]` computed at the top-level draw function into drawSelect for every mode, so this text does cycle hue with the level exactly as claimed, not a fixed yellow. No unit or macro issue; the citation and code both check out.

title: The initials-entry screen leads with an invented 'NEW HIGH SCORE'; the ROM's message is 'ENTER YOUR INITIALS' in RED

ours.verbatim: drawGlowText(ctx, 'NEW HIGH SCORE', W / 2, H * 0.2, 44, color, 24)

claim: ENTER is RED, scale 1, y=0B0h (ALLANG.MAC 69 `MESS\tENTER,RED,1,0B0\t\t;ENTER`), with the English literal 'ENTER YOUR INITIALS'. Our high-score entry screen's heading is 'NEW HIGH SCORE' in the level-cycling colour (render.ts 720); 'ENTER YOUR INITIALS' appears only in the defensive no-entry branch, and there it is drawn in CLAW_COLOR yellow (render.ts 724), not red.
reasoning: The ROM string exists in our code but is wired to a dead branch and mis-coloured, while the visible heading is invented. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALLANG.MAC:69 `MESS ENTER,RED,1,0B0 ;ENTER` and :121 `EENTER: ASCVH <ENTER YOUR INITIALS>` — both verbatim as claimed. Checked our side line by line: render.ts:720 draws the visible heading 'NEW HIGH SCORE' (not in the source's message table at all — invented) using the level-cycling `color`; the ROM string 'ENTER YOUR INITIALS' does appear in our code, but only at render.ts:724, inside the `if (!entry)` defensive branch that only fires when `s.entry` is falsy in 'highscore' mode (an edge case, not the normal path), and there it's drawn in CLAW_COLOR ('#ffe600', yellow, defined render.ts:23), not RED. Both halves of the claim (invented heading + the real ROM string demoted to a dead branch in the wrong colour) check out exactly as stated.

title: The RANK message is 'RANKING FROM 1 TO <n>', not the bare word 'RANK'
claim: The MRANK message (RED, scale 1, y=-50., ALLANG.MAC 73) has the English literal 'RANKING FROM 1 TO ' — a sentence with a trailing space onto which the game appends the top selectable level. Ours prints the four-letter label 'RANK'. The colour (RED / '#ff2f4f') is right.
reasoning: 'RANK' as a standalone caption never appeared on the cabinet; the ROM's line tells the player the range they may start from, which is the whole point of the screen. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALLANG.MAC:73 `MESS RANK,RED,1,-50. ;RANK` and :141 `ERANK: ASCVH 0C2,<RANKING FROM 1 TO >` — both verbatim as claimed (the trailing space before the closing angle bracket is real, consistent with the game appending the top selectable level number afterward). Checked our side: render.ts:701 draws the bare word 'RANK' in '#ff2f4f'; scanned the rest of drawSelect (694-715) for any other text that might append 'FROM 1 TO <n>' elsewhere on the same screen and found none — only 'SELECT START LEVEL' (not a ROM string) and 'START LEVEL NN' (the numeric readout, a different concern). The colour match is correctly conceded by the finding itself. Text-content claim holds; nothing on our screen carries the ROM's actual sentence.

title: The '(c) MCMLXXX ATARI' attract line (BLULET) is absent
claim: MATARI is BLULET, scale 1, y=92h (ALLANG.MAC 86) and its literal is identical in all four languages: '^ MCMLXXX ATARI' — where '^' is the ASCVG code for the COPYR circle-C glyph (ALVROM.MAC 31). It is one of the 30 messages. Our attract screen shows 'TEMPEST' + 'A VECTOR ARENA' (render.ts 682) and no copyright line.
reasoning: We must not ship Atari's copyright notice on a clone, and this is the only string that needs the COPYR glyph (see V-025).

title: Glyph animation frames are selected from a float render clock, not the ROM's 28.4 Hz QFRAME counter
claim: Everywhere the ROM cycles a picture it indexes QFRAME, the integer GAME-frame counter — advanced once per FRTIMR-gated MAINLN pass, i.e. 256/9 = 28.44 Hz, not once per IRQ (~256 Hz) and not 60 Hz (FR-001). Enemy shots `LDA QFRAME / ASL / AND I,6` (ALDISP.MAC 910-912), trailer/spiker `LDA QFRAME / AND I,3` (ALDISP.MAC 701-702), flippers hold one frame (`FLITAB: .BYTE CINVA1,CINVA1,CINVA1,CINVA1`, ALDISP.MAC 685). We derive frames from a float seconds clock instead: spiker `Math.floor(renderTime * 8)` (render.ts 349), fuseball `Math.floor(renderTime * 12)` (render.ts 354), pulsar strobe `Math.sin(renderTime * 12/18)` (render.ts 359-361), bullet spin `renderTime * 5` (render.ts 285). This covers only animation whose frame index is derived from TIME (a float seconds clock standing in for the ROM's integer game-frame count) — it explicitly EXCLUDES any animation whose frame index is derived from something other than elapsed time. In particular, DA-018 (pair-3-aldisp-a-objects.json) shows the enemy-bolt spin frame is computed from `Math.floor(b.depth * 8)` — the bolt's own travel DEPTH, a spatial quantity, not the render clock — so a bolt that stalls in place would freeze mid-animation; that is not a float restatement of QFRAME and is not covered by this finding.
reasoning: This is the float/dt-vs-integer-frame-counter split, not a data error: the shapes cycle, just at rates chosen in Hz rather than in frames. It only becomes a fidelity bug where the RATE matters (the pulsar's on/off duty cycle) — and note renderTime advances by a hard-coded 1/60 per RENDERED frame in render() (render.ts 905), so on a 144 Hz display the animation clock runs 2.4x fast while the sim does not. Scope note (coverage review): this STRUCTURAL/accept classification is narrowed to genuinely time-derived animation only. It must not be read as blanket cover for every glyph-animation divergence in the audit — DA-018's enemy-bolt animation is driven by travel depth, not time, and remains a live DIVERGENCE that this finding does not excuse. Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-017, and FR-001, in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json): QFRAME ticks at 28.44 Hz, not 60, so any animation rate here that was picked to imitate a ROM frame cadence is 2.11x fast at 60 Hz (and 5x on a 144 Hz panel, since the clock rides the render loop) — the accept covers the float/integer split, not the base.
